steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team concluded that differences in anatomical features (head shape) may affect dogs' ability to distinguish speech from nonspeech. Supporting evidence must show that dogs with different anatomy perform differently on the speech-vs-nonspeech task, not on any other task.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Compared with longer-headed dogs, shorter-headed dogs showed less difference in brain activity when hearing either Spanish or Hungarian than when hearing the scrambled recording.: This directly links head shape to performance on the speech-vs-nonspeech discrimination: longer-headed dogs differentiate more, shorter-headed dogs less. ✓
  • Choice B — Compared with longer-headed dogs, shorter-headed dogs showed a greater difference in brain activity when hearing the language they were accustomed to than when hearing the other language.: This compares familiar language vs. unfamiliar language — language discrimination, not speech-vs-nonspeech discrimination. ✗
  • Choice C — The pattern of brain activity that long-headed dogs showed when hearing the scrambled recording was different from the pattern of brain activity that short-headed dogs showed when hearing the language they were accustomed to.: This compares two different conditions (scrambled for one group, familiar speech for another) across groups, which does not isolate head shape as the variable affecting speech-nonspeech detection. ✗
  • Choice D — Long-headed dogs accustomed to hearing Spanish tended to show more brain activity when hearing Spanish than long-headed dogs accustomed to hearing Hungarian showed when hearing Hungarian.: Compares only long-headed dogs to each other based on which language they know; head shape is not varied and speech-vs-nonspeech is not tested. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
